questions.text: I am prone to sudden surges of emotion that catch me off guard.
I find that completing something meaningful produces a high so intense it borders on euphoria.
I feel that the weight of suffering in my moral thinking is the feature that ensures my values remain living commitments rather than static principles.
I believe that keeping emotional composure when others are hurting enables me to think through the problem more thoroughly.
I find that I am genuinely puzzled when people tell me I missed an obvious social cue.
I tend to be unaware of the subtle ways people express loyalty to each other in group settings.
I believe that building a personal portfolio of accomplishments is essential.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is a force that operates on me whether I want it to or not, and I am glad that it does.
I find that I am surprised when people read a lot into minor changes in my behavior.
I sense when the energy of a room drops the moment a particular person starts speaking.
I notice that my emotional life, while not dramatic, is dependable and clear.
I believe it's important to celebrate my own accomplishments.
I often position myself as the final decision-maker in group settings.
I think that the discipline of maintaining emotional steadiness during exposure to suffering is a practice that yields benefits for everyone involved.
I trust that important dates will come to mind when they need to.
I can receive criticism without it sending a jolt through my system.
I routinely gather extra supplies so I can offer them to anyone in my circle who runs short.
I pick up on when a person lingers after a group disperses because they want a private word with someone specific.
I pick up on the specific way that silence falls after someone says something that crossed a line.
I keep important documents filed and easy to locate at a moment's notice.
I enforce only standards the group has explicitly agreed to.
I think of time as something to be experienced rather than optimized.
I believe that the ability to manage my emotional state during crises is a skill I have developed intentionally and continue to refine.
I tend to be the one who says the meeting is over and assigns next steps.
I am drawn to situations where my voice carries significant weight.
I hold the view that people should ensure their own stability before worrying about others.
I tend to absorb costs and difficulties so that others are shielded from them.
I wake up some mornings already buzzing with emotional energy before anything has happened.
I invest time in building habits that will pay dividends over months and years.
I feel that committing to a direction should be a collective act.
I believe that building sustainable systems is more important than achieving quick wins.
I let brainstorming run its natural course before anyone pushes toward execution.
I pick up paused projects and re-orient myself through exploration rather than notes.
I tend to donate or share resources before setting aside savings for myself.
I believe that the moral weight of another's suffering is the single most compelling reason to change what I am doing.
I feel that I have a naturally wide window of tolerance for emotional stimulation.
